U.K. PETROLEUM PRODUCT stocks fell sharply in the first two months of the war between the United States and Iran, according to official statistics released for the first time today. By the end of April, gasoline stocks had depleted to the lowest for fourteen years and diesel stocks were at the lowest for more than two decades. Similar depletion is likely to have occurred in most of the other major economies, putting intense pressure behind the scenes on policymakers to find a way to re-open the Strait of Hormuz:

EIA’s petroleum supply monthly report for April showed ending US commercial crude storage at 451.997 million bbls versus EIA’s 457.812 million bbls. 

This means that the incoming weekly inventory reports will have to adjust inventory down by 5.185 million bbls.

The delta is larger, 7.639 million bbls.
It’s kind of obvious that it will be from China.

Can't believe half of 2026 is already in the rearview mirror. Time for the month end and H1 review.

As my followers already know, I took a massive punch to the jaw in my crude book from mid May to mid June, puking back a huge chunk of my YTD P/L.

The year started out absolute money with monster gains in precious metals and copper. I even built a massive long crude position during a sleepless night in Tel Aviv right before the Iran war kicked off.

From there, I rode the prompt futures rollover train. It was pure alpha through the April, May, and June Brent expiries, but the July contract broke me.

I tried to pivot out to the long dateds and structured options, but the sheer velocity of the crude selloff blew right past my max MDD. I had to rip the band aid off, cut the positions, and hand back roughly 70% of my cumulative H1 oil profits.

My macro framework on crude is still a total mess of conflicting signals:

First, can China really keep up this charade forever? Yeah, I get the consensus take that they have massive buffers and are just burning down excess inventory. But their energy security bottleneck remains a glaring geopolitical Achilles' heel. Extrapolating inventory baselines from years ago is a broken thesis—the reality is that China’s baseline crude appetite has structurally expanded.

Second, if you do the napkin math, global aggregate inventories are sitting below pre-war levels despite the historic SPR dumps. This whole episode just proved how massive the SPR weapon is, which means they’ll have to reload the clip eventually. So what happens to the tape when that structural bid hits the market?

Third, why on earth is Beijing running a playbook that deepens their own domestic deflationary pain just to bail out the American consumer? I’m starting to think this isn't an economic trade at all—it smells like a structural play to lock down state control over the domestic refining complex, or a full-blown dress rehearsal for a Taiwan contingency.

Fourth, getting your frozen funds back and dodging sanctions is a sweet deal. For Tehran, securing that without giving up an inch on their proxies or missile tech is a technical win. But Iran had the leverage to push Trump to the absolute brink and force a blowout midterm defeat, teaching the world a permanent lesson about trying to invade them. Why leave all that leverage on the floor?

I have a million other questions, but ending the final month of the half deep in the red tells me everything I need to know—I completely lost my feel for the oil tape. The only discipline that matters here is checking my ego, admitting I'm out of sync, and staying flat crude.

Since mid June, I aggressively loaded into a short EURUSD book, which helped patch up some of the bleeding from the crude disaster. The thesis was clean: the Eurozone's macro fundamentals are completely hollowed out, meaning ECB tightening would just trigger growth panics rather than the textbook rate hike currency bump, chocking out the Euro.

Luckily the FOMC accelerated the flush ahead of schedule, and I hit the bid to lock in profits. There might be more downside left in the tank, but I closed out the entire book last week bc I felt the market had priced in an overly aggressive Fed path that was stretching reality.

Right now, I’ve layered into some precious metals longs and used Monday’s flush to buy Nikkei futures. I’m not calling the absolute bottom in gold, and I know the immediate tape is facing downside pressure.

But all this panic about the new Fed Chair Warsh being a rabid hawk feels like a narrative that’s going to run out of gas. The market has just run way too far ahead of the data.

I understand the structural case for curve flattening, but even if the US economy keeps printing hot, inflation data is highly likely to blow up this hawkish path. Right now, the 'Transitory' playbook still looks like the higher-probability trade.

So, while I don't know where the absolute floor for gold is, trying to time it perfectly is a loser's game. With absolute doom-posting flooding the space and the weak hands getting completely rinsed out, this looks like a prime window to accumulate long exposure on the cheap. 

The structural long-term trend is ironclad. I’m looking to layer into this setup slowly, keeping a worst-case flush down to 3,500 on my radar. If this plays out right, it could turn into a massive print.

The Nikkei is a purely tactical, fast-money trade. The KOSPI is delivering absolute garbage vol right now—a total untradable tape. 

Japan has a fundamental story with solid momentum, and I’m going to keep riding the index until JPY vol wakes up and breaks the setup. Long-term, I’m still looking to camp out in individual Japanese equity names.

Honestly the current board feels pretty barren, which sucks. The real alpha right now should be in stock-picking and sector rotation, but my equity book is strictly locked down for long-term investing. 

H1 started with a bang and ended with a whimper, but we reset and look for cleaner execution in H2.

Good luck out there to the whole desk, and enjoy the Fourth of July weekend!

Gold's rebound above USD 4,000 appears to reflect a combination of short covering after the break below last week's low at USD 3,960 failed to attract sustained follow-through selling, together with some bottom fishing as energy prices continue to ease amid the risk of a short-term glut helping to ease inflation worries further. While the recovery may increase confidence in holding longs, prices first need to break above USD 4,100 before it is reasonable to consider that a low may have been established.

#Commodities update:

Chicago #grain futures slumped ahead of today's closely watched U.S. Department of Agriculture acreage and quarterly grain stocks reports, with traders largely looking past near-term forecasts for a hot spell across the U.S. Midwest. Quarterly #corn inventories are projected to reach their highest level since 1988, up 17% from a year ago. Attention is also focused on updated planting estimates following a generally favourable spring season. #Wheat remained under pressure as rapid U.S. harvest progress and expectations for another large Black Sea crop continued to weigh on prices. Reflecting the deteriorating sentiment, hedge funds have sold more than 600,000 contracts across corn, soybeans and wheat in just a few weeks, flipping from a four-year high net long to a net short of 104,000 contracts.

#CrudeOil prices traded within a relatively tight range near recent lows while remaining on track for their biggest quarterly decline since the pandemic. Flows through the Strait of Hormuz continue to accelerate, prompting warnings from Morgan Stanley that the release of previously stranded barrels could create a near-term supply glut and put additional downward pressure on prices. WTI traded near USD 70 a barrel, while the soon-to-expire August Brent contract hovered around USD 72.

#Gold's latest rebound attempt has once again run out of steam, with prices slipping back below USD 4,000. The failure to sustain gains highlights the current fragile sentiment, where traders continue to sell into strength rather than buy into weakness, a notable shift from the behaviour seen over the past few years. The macro backdrop remains dominated by ongoing U.S. dollar strength - with USD/JPY reaching its highest level since 1986 in Asia today - and lingering concerns that the Federal Reserve may keep a hawkish stance despite the sharp decline in energy prices.

#Copper trades steady ahead of today’s deadline on a potential 15% tariff announcement on US refined copper imports, with warnings that higher costs from a copper tariff could destroy more demand than they create and harm US manufacturers, as it would come on top of existing 50% tariffs on semi-finished copper products and derivatives.

text:
今日美股总结：今天反弹不简单 --- 养老金最后两天的抛售、华尔街的洗盘、PCE统计改了、韩国砸5180亿搞半导体

华尔街是怎么洗盘的
开盘前45分钟，机构大幅打压半导体板块制造恐慌，诱导散户抛售割肉转投医疗保健和七巨头。散户离场之后，机构在低位迅速接盘大举涌入，许多半导体股票随后走出死里逃生式的V型反转，甚至演变成暴涨。半导体收了上下影线的股票都算是final capitulation因为大部分散户投降了。
美光和闪迪回踩EMA20后精准反弹，重演了历史上的经典走势，财报公布后的走势属于典型的跌破前低、迅速拉回、回测确认、向上突破的教科书级洗盘。
ARM盘中一度跌到300美元附近的EMA50实施剧烈洗盘，最终大幅收涨43美元。
今天最抗跌、走势最好的两个板块分别是半导体设备和网络安全。网络安全这条线值得单独说一下，AI的发展不会消除安全问题，反而会制造更多隐患。 AMAT和CRWD可以关注。

地缘政治这边又有进展，是今天反弹的主要催化剂之一，虽然这部分影响已经非常小了
美国和伊朗同意停止互相攻击，特朗普说伊朗已请求在多哈会面，卡塔尔即将解冻伊朗60亿美元的资产。
原油这边，目前油价已经较近期高点回落了40.95%。如果地缘和谈顺利，油价可能进一步承压。我自己的判断是原油今年中期选举年不用太担心了，120是今年的顶，通胀也会跟着下来。
7月4日美国250周年国庆前夕，除非出现极端搅局者，地缘政治大概率会迎来几天的太平日子，短期避险情绪会回落。

今天大盘集体跳空高开超过1%，这个信号的历史统计也值得放进来
历史数据表明，大盘当天集体向上跳空超过1%是极度看涨的信号：跳空2天后上涨概率75%，2周后86%，3周和1个月后上涨概率甚至达到100%，1个月后的平均回报率可达6.7%。
这个数据跟之前一直在讲的季节性规律是吻合的。明天是6月最后一天，历史数据表明7月通常是美股表现最强劲的月份之一，尤其是过去20年的统计里表现最好。

本周关键日程，周四非农是重点，但我不是很担心
周二关注JOLTs职位空缺数据。周三美联储官员发表讲话。周四非农就业报告和失业率数据，是本周最重要的经济事件。周五7月3日因独立日假期休市。
我不担心周四这个数据，白宫首席经济顾问凯文·哈萨特在非农发布前公开发言，说目前看到的指标表明就业报告会是一个强劲但温和的数字，不存在过热失控的态势。6月新增非农大幅超预期的可能性不大，5月超预期主要是因为世界杯的短暂影响导致企业提前招工，这是个一次性因素，不是趋势。

特斯拉今天暴涨8.5%，技术面值得说一下
长期处于下降通道里，短期要关注413到415这个阻力区间，一旦放量突破，有望直接上攻通道上轨420甚至433下跌趋势线第四次碰到的这个位置这个目标位。2026年可能引入北美的Model Y长轴版的传闻也带来了一些情绪催化。

技术面分析
明天SPY的每日隐含动能范围大约是上轨746，下轨735，如果大盘回落，此前的阻力位会转换为支撑，可能在735附近砸出更高的低点，这也是下跌趋势线的突破回踩，形成非常理想的右侧多头回测买点。
市场正在从高波动的环境逐步过渡到牛市低波动环境。标普已经重新夺回并站上了Gamma翻转线之上，处于正Gamma状态。
VIX今天下跌4.13%回落到17.65。从VIX图表能清楚看出，价格重回翻转线上方时，VIX受到剧烈压制，意味着市场重回买入回踩、抛售冲高的趋势平稳期。10年期美债小幅回落0.05%，美元指数横盘震荡。
这些都给美股创造了很好的上涨环境。

PCE通胀统计口径被修改了
电脑软件和配件这一项加入了游戏软件的复合指数，因为游戏类产品价格长期在往下走，这一项单独就能把5月核心PCE拉低0.1到0.2个百分点。
投资组合管理服务这块改用就业薪资增速来算，不再用资产价格做基准，因为行业薪资增长比较温和，这一项的通胀从21.6%直接砍到9%，单项就能让核心PCE下修0.21个百分点。
法律服务这块停用了一个长期没更新的单一口径，换成了更细分的复合指数，同比涨幅从7.6%降到4.25%。
这几项加起来，5月核心PCE同比直接被下修0.2个百分点到3.2%，2026年12月的核心PCE全年预期也从3.2%调到了3.0%。瑞银提醒说这种改法会让数据的透明度变差，存在被操纵的嫌疑，但市场现在只关心结果是利好：加息的必要性进一步弱化，降息的预期反而升温了。

业绩基本面这边，高盛上调了AI企业的盈利预期，这是支撑反弹的核心理由
高盛最新的策略报告说，标普二季度整体盈利有望大涨22%。背后两个推动力：一是AI资本开支继续爆，光英伟达和微软两家公司对整体盈利增长的贡献就超过40%；二是AI的需求正在从单纯的算力端往终端消费、企业软件这些更大的场景里扩散。

韩国砸5180亿美元搞半导体国家战略，市场的反应挺反常的，要讲清楚为什么
韩国总统宣布五年内要把本国存储芯片产能翻一倍，这种规模的扩产消息按常理应该会让市场担心未来供给过剩，结果市场反而涨了。背后有几层逻辑：
第一，时间上变量太大。真正的产能落地是好几年甚至十年后的事，这只是个政策方向，根本改变不了未来一两年全球HBM和通用存储紧缺的实际局面。
第二，增量需求把扩产的担忧给抵消了。韩国不只是在扩存储产能，还在往本地AI算力集群和数据中心砸巨额资金，这些基础设施建起来之后会产生大量的存储硬件采购需求，正好把存储厂自己扩产带来的供给风险给平衡掉了。
第三，真正赚得最稳的是上游设备商。韩国这次的投资，对中短期的存储供需格局影响是中性的，但晶圆厂和封装厂要建起来，必须海量采购设备，这对AMAT、Lam Research、ASML这些上游设备公司是确定性极高的长期增量利好，跟之前讲半导体设备股那篇的逻辑是同一条线。

总结一下我对今天的判断
今天的反弹不是单一原因撑起来的，是机构洗盘、地缘缓和、非农预期被提前管理、PCE统计口径被系统性下修、AI盈利预期上调、韩国扩产带动设备股增量利好，这几件事凑在一起同时发生。
跳空超过1%之后的历史胜率很高，季末的Gamma结构也回到了正值区间，市场正在从偏脆弱的状态慢慢过渡到相对稳定的偏多环境。后天即便非农数据本身不弱，市场的预期也已经被提前安抚过了，应该不太容易引发真正的恐慌。
操作上继续按计划分批接，半导体设备和网络安全今天走得最稳，值得多关注关注。存储这条线的洗盘基本已经走完了，回踩均线精准反弹说明这波抛压已经被消化得差不多了。

Daily Market Wrap 01/07/2026

US equity markets extended their recent rally overnight, capping one of the strongest quarterly performances in recent years. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq both recorded their best quarterly gains since 2020 as investors continued to rotate into growth stocks on expectations that the US economy remains resilient despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty.

Treasury yields moved sharply higher following stronger-than-expected US JOLTS Job Openings data, which pointed to continued strength in the labour market. The 2-year Treasury yield rose 6.8 basis points to 4.172%, while the benchmark 10-year yield climbed 9.1 basis points to 4.465%. The US dollar also edged modestly higher, although price action remained volatile throughout the session and USDJPY notably hit another fresh record high.

Commodity markets were comparatively subdued. Oil prices eased slightly as traders continued to monitor developments surrounding negotiations between the United States and Iran, with hopes that diplomatic progress could help reduce geopolitical risks and support supply expectations. Brent crude settled 0.31% lower at $72.92 per barrel, while WTI crude declined 0.92% to $70.08. Gold also finished marginally lower, ending the session at $4,006.89 an ounce after briefly trading at another fresh annual low earlier in the day.

Attention now turns to another busy session for global markets, with central bank speakers taking centre stage at the ECB's annual forum in Sintra. Comments from ECB President Christine Lagarde, Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh, Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey and Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem will be closely monitored for any guidance on the outlook for interest rates. Investors will also remain focused on the US labour market, with ADP employment figures and the ISM Manufacturing PMI expected to provide further insight ahead of Friday's closely watched Non-Farm Payrolls report.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 1, 2026

Market Brief

Central Political Bureau of the CPC holds meeting to study flood and drought control measures

China's manufacturing PMI rises to 50.3% in June, returning to expansion territory

Eight ministries: industrial internet core industry value to exceed 2.5 trillion yuan by 2030

CSRC: firmly eliminate conditions enabling financial fraud in capital markets

Middle East tensions escalate as Iran and Qatar deny arranging high-level U.S.-Iran talks

U.S. Supreme Court blocks Trump's executive order limiting birthright citizenship

Yen hits 40-year low against dollar; Japan may intervene abruptly

First-half trading ends: A-share turnover hits record high; Korea's Kospi leads global gains

Top Stories

The Central Political Bureau of the Communist Party of China held a meeting on June 30 to study flood and drought control measures. The meeting noted that extreme weather events will remain frequent this main flood season, with both droughts and floods likely. All regions and departments must uphold a bottom-line and worst-case mindset, prepare for major floods, severe droughts, and powerful typhoons, and take robust measures to ensure flood control, drought response, and emergency rescue operations.

The China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) held a meeting on June 30 to advance coordinated efforts between central and local authorities to combat financial fraud in capital markets. CSRC Chairman Wu Qing emphasized the strategic importance of building a comprehensive punishment and prevention system and stressed the collective political responsibility to jointly eradicate the conditions that enable financial fraud. The CSRC will continue strengthening communication and collaboration with local authorities to foster a market environment where fraud is "unthinkable, unfeasible, and undesirable."

Tensions in the Middle East have further complicated. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Nasser Kanaani stated that Iran has "no plans for any level of talks with the U.S. in the coming days." He noted that planned activities in Doha on July 1 would focus on implementing a memorandum of understanding. Qatari Foreign Ministry spokesperson Ansari confirmed that a U.S. presidential envoy and Trump's son-in-law visited Qatar but did not include direct negotiations with Iran.

The U.S. Supreme Court ruled against the Trump administration's executive order restricting birthright citizenship. However, President Trump stated that Congress should pass legislation to end the "expensive" and "unfair" policy. Birthright citizenship has existed in the U.S. for over 150 years. Trump signed the executive order on his first day back in office in January, stipulating that children born to parents who are neither U.S. citizens nor legal permanent residents cannot automatically acquire U.S. citizenship.

The yen fell below the key 162 level against the dollar for the first time since December 1986, sparking market speculation over whether Japanese authorities will intervene. Finance Minister Kōtō Katayama reiterated Japan's readiness to act but used cautious language, avoiding a strong intervention signal. Most institutions believe even if Japan intervenes, it is unlikely to reverse the yen's long-term depreciation trend. Traders are increasingly shifting the perceived intervention threshold to 163 or lower.

China Macro & Policy

The National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) convened a field meeting to promote the development of private enterprises, emphasizing further improvements in the business environment and services to boost high-quality private investment. During the 15th Five-Year Plan period, the combined output value of China's six emerging pillar industries is expected to exceed 10 trillion yuan, with service sector growth potential reaching 20 trillion yuan. Accelerated implementation of "six major networks" and key infrastructure projects will drive investment allocation.

China's first administrative regulation on outward investment — "State Council Regulations on Outward Investment" — will take effect on July 1. The regulation extends coverage to individual investors and impacts reverse investments via red-chip structures and other forms of outward direct investment aimed at acquiring equity or control.

China's economic activity shows signs of recovery. Data released by the National Bureau of Statistics showed that China's manufacturing PMI rose to 50.3% in June, up 0.3 percentage points from May, returning to expansion territory. The non-manufacturing PMI and composite PMI output index rose to 50.2% and 50.6%, respectively, each up 0.1 percentage point. The high-tech manufacturing PMI reached 53.5%, up 0.6 percentage points, significantly outperforming the overall manufacturing sector, highlighting sustained strength in advanced manufacturing.

Data from the Ministry of Finance showed that from January to May, state-owned enterprises recorded a total operating revenue of 32.82 trillion yuan, a 0.7% year-on-year decline, while total profits reached 1.73 trillion yuan, up 3.5% year-on-year. As of the end of May, the average asset-liability ratio of state-owned enterprises stood at 65.5%, up 0.5 percentage points year-on-year.

China Equities Markets

A-shares rose amid volatility. The SSE Index closed up 0.5% at 4,094.4 points, the Shenzhen Component Index gained 2.48%, the ChiNext Index surged 2.99%, and the Star 50 Index jumped 3.85%. The Wind All China Index rose 1.69%, with market turnover reaching 3.29 trillion yuan. Semiconductor supply chain, computing hardware, and robotics sectors led gains. The first half of the year concluded with the Star 50 Index up nearly 64% and the ChiNext Index up over 35%. Total market turnover surpassed 317.5 trillion yuan, setting a new record for a six-month period. Semiconductor equipment, CPO, and PCB sectors posted the strongest gains. 

Hong Kong equities diverged. The Hang Seng Index fell 0.63% to 22,881.02 points, the Hang Seng Tech Index rose 1.8%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index slipped 0.62%. CPO, chip, and large model concepts outperformed, while financial and consumer stocks weakened. Net inflows via the Southbound Stock Connect reached nearly 5.9 billion Hong Kong dollars, with SMIC receiving over 1.9 billion Hong Kong dollars in net purchases. For the first half of the year, the Hang Seng Index fell more than 10%, and the Hang Seng Tech Index declined nearly 19%, both hitting recent lows.

Taiwan's Weighted Index closed up 2.5% at 46,125.91 points, up over 59% for the first half of the year. AI-related stocks surged, with MediaTek rising over 8% and gaining more than 200% in the first half. TSMC rose about 1.7%, up over 55% for the first half.

The Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited implemented a simplified board lot framework, lowering the minimum board lot value from HK$2,000 to HK$1,000. A maximum board lot value of HK$50,000 was introduced for lots exceeding 100 shares. Standardized board lot sizes are now limited to 1, 50, 100, 500, 1,000, 2,000, 5,000, and 10,000 shares.

In June, three stocks on broker gold lists — Yake Technology, Guocai Materials, and International Composite Materials — more than doubled in value. Over the first half of the year, broker gold lists delivered significant outperformance. Ping An Securities' gold portfolio recorded the highest return at 47.83%. Recent July gold lists show AI supply chain remains the core allocation theme, with growing attention to pharmaceuticals and brokerage sectors. Institutions generally maintain long-term confidence in technology stocks but increasingly call for near-term rebalancing.

China Industry News

The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and seven other ministries jointly issued an opinion on promoting high-quality development of the industrial internet. By 2030, the core industry value of the industrial internet is expected to exceed 2.5 trillion yuan, with integration applications covering all 207 industrial subcategories. New infrastructure will be deployed at scale, including 50,000 industrial 5G private networks. Identifier resolution services will expand to more enterprises, five globally influential comprehensive platforms will be built, and a preliminary industrial data governance system will be established.

Multiple sources indicate that several Chinese smartphone manufacturers have again lowered their 2026 shipment targets due to ongoing component shortages and rising costs. Xiaomi has further reduced its forecast by about 30% to approximately 95 million units, while OPPO and vivo have lowered their projections to below 90 million units each.

Data from the State Administration of Taxation showed that from January to May, revenue from enterprises in the embodied intelligence industry rose 22.4% year-on-year. Revenue growth for robot manufacturing, AI algorithms and software integration, and system integration and industry applications reached 30.1%, 24.5%, and 27.9%, respectively.

According to the China Index Academy, total sales by the top 100 real estate developers reached 1.59 trillion yuan in the first half of the year, with the year-on-year decline narrowing by 1.3 percentage points from January to May. June single-month sales rose 11.8% month-on-month. Sales of premium products in first-tier cities increased by more than 10 percentage points, while first-time and upgrade demand grew in third- and fourth-tier cities.

Ma Xiaoli, deputy director of the National Power Battery Innovation Center, said the domestic battery industry is transitioning from "high-speed growth" to "steady growth and structural upgrading" due to slowing growth in new energy vehicle sales. Domestic battery demand is projected to reach 888.7 GWh in 2026, up 15.8% year-on-year.

The China Academy of Information and Communications Technology (CAICT) launched the "Token of Computing Power Overseas Ecosystem Initiative," aiming to advance China's computing power token industry from exploratory beginnings toward standardized, efficient, and sustainable high-quality development through three pillars: assessing trends, building ecosystems, and optimizing pathways.

Fintech

China UnionPay, in collaboration with Ping An Bank and Tencent Cloud, launched the "AI Smart Compute Card," a debit product targeting individual AI computing consumers. Core benefits no longer include points for airline miles, video memberships, or physical goods, but instead offer large model token packages, GPU inference hours, and access to AI productivity and learning tools.

Andrew Bailey, Deputy Governor of the Bank of England, warned that autonomous AI agents could trigger "market crashes" and may require stricter regulation. He noted that if such agents respond identically to similar prompts, they could amplify volatility during periods of market stress.

The transition period for the EU's Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) ended on July 1. All cryptocurrency exchanges without a CASP license must fully cease operations targeting European residents, with no grace period for wind-downs. Violators face fines or criminal sanctions.

A consortium supported by BlackRock, Google, and Coinbase plans to launch a stablecoin called "OpenUSD," expected to go live later this year. Around 140 companies, including payment giants Visa, Stripe, and Mastercard, have signed on to use the stablecoin.

JPMorgan Chase's blockchain subsidiary Kinexys expanded its global Blockchain Deposit Account (BDA) network to include accounts denominated in Australian dollar, Hong Kong dollar, Japanese yen, Chinese yuan, and Singapore dollar.

Corporate News & Updates

UBTECH Robotics unveiled its full-sized ultra-realistic humanoid robot series, U1, including the U1 Lite (half-body), U1 Pro (full-body high-end), and U1 Ultra (full-body high-dynamic). The U1 Lite is priced at 119,800 yuan; the U1 Pro at 169,800 yuan; the male U1 Ultra at 990,000 yuan and the female version at 880,000 yuan.

Samsung's wafer foundry business is reviving its 1.4nm process development. According to Korean media, Samsung has not abandoned the 1.4nm node as previously rumored but has delayed its timeline, pushing the mass production target from 2027 to 2029.

The UK's Competition and Markets Authority proposed allowing app developers to direct users to payment channels outside Apple and Google's app stores to reduce fees and promote competition. The authority is also considering requiring Apple to open access to its near-field communication (NFC) technology.

Sources said OpenAI engineers have developed new technical optimizations capable of reducing model inference costs by more than 50%.

Meituan unveiled its next-generation foundational large model, LongCat-2.0, and plans to open-source it soon. It is the world's first trillion-parameter model trained and inferred entirely on domestic computing power, with training and inference costs lower than other global trillion-parameter models.

Moonshot AI completed its recent $20 billion valuation funding round and has launched a new round with a pre-money valuation of $31.5 billion. As of mid-June, Kimi's ARR (annual recurring revenue) exceeded $300 million.

Global Macro & Policy

U.S. President Trump's 2025 financial disclosure revealed he holds at least $5 million in Apple stock, $5 million in NVIDIA stock, and $1 million in Palantir stock, and generated over $1 billion in income from the cryptocurrency industry.

Cleveland Fed President Lynn Browne said she sees little evidence that interest rates are restraining the economy and suggested the Fed may need to raise rates further to bring inflation down to its 2% target.

U.S. Labor Department data showed job openings rose slightly to 7.594 million in May, exceeding market expectations of 7.3 million and marking the fifth consecutive month above forecasts. The quits rate held steady at 1.9%, layoffs increased slightly, and hiring remained flat.

The Conference Board reported that U.S. consumer confidence rose to 91.2 in June, up from a revised 90.6 in May, driven by lower oil prices easing inflation concerns. However, consumers' views on the labor market weakened significantly.

NASA announced new lunar base project plans, signing contracts worth nearly $600 million with three U.S. companies to execute four new lunar landing missions by the end of 2028.

Preliminary data showed Germany's June CPI rose 2.3% year-on-year, France's at 1.8%, and Italy's at 3%, all below expectations. ECB policymaker Vítor Constâncio said the case for another rate hike is less compelling than in June.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indices closed higher. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.26% to 52,319.2, the S&P 500 gained 0.79% to 7,499.36, and the Nasdaq Composite jumped 1.52% to 26,213.72. Caterpillar rose over 3%, Apple gained over 2%, leading the Dow. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index rose 1.43%, with NVIDIA and Tesla up over 2%. SpaceX rose over 4%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index rose 0.63%, with Hesaitech up over 15% and Canadian Solar up over 8.5%. In June, the Dow rose 2.52%, the S&P 500 fell 1.06%, and the Nasdaq fell 2.81%. For the first half of the year, the Dow rose 8.85%, the S&P 500 rose 9.55%, and the Nasdaq rose 12.79%.

European major indices closed higher. The German DAX rose 1.50% to 24,995.81, the French CAC 40 gained 0.44% to 8,403.99, and the FTSE 100 rose 0.12% to 10,497.12. France's June inflation fell below expectations to 2%, easing ECB rate hike pressure, while JPMorgan's upgrade of European equities year-end targets and a rally in chip stocks lifted risk appetite. In June, the DAX fell 0.43%, the CAC 40 rose 2.7%, and the FTSE 100 rose 0.84%. For the first half of the year, the DAX rose 2.06%, the CAC 40 rose 3.12%, and the FTSE 100 rose 5.7%.

The Korea Composite Stock Price Index (Kospi) closed up 0.97% at 8,476.48. Benefiting from the AI trading boom, the Kospi rose over 100% in the first half of the year, leading global major indices. The Nikkei 225 rose 0.86% to 70,062.32, up over 39% for the first half. The S&P BSE SENSEX fell 0.33% to 76,478.67.

South Korean financial regulators and the Korea Exchange are drafting reforms for the KOSDAQ market, with plans to establish a core index system for top-tier segments. Authorities are advancing an upgrade/downgrade system to restructure KOSDAQ into three tiers: Select, Standard, and Management categories.

CME Group announced it will launch single-stock futures on July 27, pending regulatory approval. The initial lineup includes 55 standard contracts and 22 micro contracts covering over 50 U.S. popular stocks, including Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, NVIDIA, and SpaceX.

Sources said SpaceX is considering donating stock to a new child savings plan named the "Trump Account." The White House has positioned the "Trump Account" as a flagship economic initiative, with a launch event planned next week involving the New York Stock Exchange and Nasdaq.

SK Hynix has officially filed for a U.S. IPO, seeking to list American Depositary Shares (ADS) on the Nasdaq Global Select Market. Each ADS will represent a specified number of SK Hynix ordinary shares. Key terms such as issuance size, price, and ADS ratio remain undisclosed.

Nike reported fourth-quarter fiscal 2026 revenue of $10.97 billion, down 1.1% year-on-year. Nike brand revenue was $10.72 billion, down 0.4%. EBIT in Greater China was $243 million, down 20%. Gross margin rose to 49.2% from 40.3% a year ago. EPS was $0.72 compared to $0.14 in the prior-year quarter.

According to SEC filings, NVIDIA shareholders approved a proposal to change the "supermajority voting rule" to a "simple majority voting standard." Shareholders also approved a non-binding advisory vote on executive compensation.

Bonds

China's interbank bond market cooled significantly, with major interest rate bond yields rising by more than 1 basis point. Treasury futures declined, with the 30-year contract down 0.49%. The People's Bank of China conducted 69.5 billion yuan in 7-day reverse repo operations and 600 billion yuan in overnight reverse repo operations, net withdrawing 155 billion yuan. Liquidity pressure in the interbank market remained limited.

The Ministry of Finance announced its third-quarter treasury issuance schedule: ultra-long-term special treasury bonds will begin issuance on July 3, with 11 issues total — three 20-year, six 30-year, and two 50-year bonds.

The Shanghai Stock Exchange strengthened investor suitability requirements for bonds and enhanced monitoring of abnormal trading. For interest rate bonds, trades with prices deviating more than 2% from current valuations, and for credit bonds (excluding special bonds) with deviations exceeding 5%, customers must provide secondary confirmation.

U.S. Treasury yields rose across the curve. The 2-year yield climbed 7.20 basis points to 4.174%, the 3-year yield rose 7.65 basis points to 4.181%, the 5-year yield increased 8.76 basis points to 4.228%, the 10-year yield rose 9.27 basis points to 4.465%, and the 30-year yield climbed 9.03 basis points to 4.953%.

Commodities

The Shanghai Futures Exchange and Shanghai International Energy Exchange announced adjustments to price limits and margin requirements for multiple futures contracts effective from the close of trading on July 2. Gold futures price limits are set at 14%, with margin requirements at 15% for hedging positions and 16% for general positions. Copper, aluminum, zinc, lead, alumina futures, and international copper futures have price limits of 9%, with margin requirements of 10% and 11% respectively.

International precious metals closed mixed. COMEX gold futures fell 0.42% to $4,021.80 per ounce, down 12.44% in June; COMEX silver futures rose 0.70% to $59.05 per ounce, down 22.76% in June. Hawkish Fed rate hike expectations weighed on the financial appeal of precious metals, while the absence of escalated U.S.-Iran tensions limited safe-haven demand, leaving only industrially driven commodities relatively resilient.

U.S. crude oil futures fell 1.02% to $70.03 per barrel, down 19% in June; Brent crude futures dropped 0.65% to $73.43 per barrel, down 19.30% in June. The rapid restoration of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz following a tentative U.S.-Iran agreement, coupled with warnings from multiple institutions of global oil supply glut, triggered the price decline.

London Metal Exchange base metals mostly rose. LME tin rose 2.53% to $51,650 per ton, down 6.8% in June; LME zinc rose 2.22% to $3,551.5 per ton, up 0.32% in June; LME copper rose 0.76% to $13,380 per ton, down 1.88% in June; LME aluminum rose 0.55% to $3,105 per ton, down 15.31% in June; LME nickel fell 0.01% to $16,310 per ton, down 14.44% in June; LME lead fell 1.14% to $1,872 per ton, down 7.14% in June.

Russian Presidential Press Secretary Dmitry Peskov said Russia is in discussions with certain countries regarding the possibility of importing petroleum products. If acceptable pricing can be reached, Russia will import petroleum products to stabilize its domestic energy market.

FX & Currency Markets

On Tuesday, the onshore renminbi against the dollar closed at 6.7852, up 77 basis points from the previous trading day. For the first half of the year, it appreciated by 2,038 basis points. The offshore rate closed at 6.7880. The central parity rate was set at 6.8109, up 66 basis points from the previous day, with a cumulative appreciation of 2,179 basis points in the first half.

At New York close, the U.S. Dollar Index rose 0.06% to 101.17, up 2.27% for the month. Non-U.S. currencies were mixed: the euro fell 0.02% to 1.1421, the pound rose 0.01% to 1.3260, the Australian dollar rose 0.48% to 0.6920, the dollar-yen rose 0.40% to 162.5760, the dollar-loonie fell 0.10% to 1.4197, the dollar-franc rose 0.11% to 0.8084, and the offshore renminbi rose 96 basis points to 6.7913.

The Bank of Korea sold a net $13.628 billion in foreign exchange interventions in the first quarter to curb won depreciation — its sixth consecutive quarter of dollar sales.

SECTOR AND MACRO INTERPRETATION

The sector table shows Industrials, Information Technology, and Energy as the 3 strongest sectors at 19.5 percent, 19.4 percent, and 18.0 percent, respectively. The combination is economically coherent: Industrials capture physical infrastructure, IT captures AI compute and semiconductor supply chains, and Energy captures geopolitical supply shock plus power-demand sensitivity. The weaker sectors are Financials at negative 2.1 percent, Consumer Discretionary at negative 1.1 percent, and Communication Services at 0.4 percent. This pattern is consistent with an economy where nominal capex, supply constraints, and AI infrastructure spending dominate, while rate-sensitive, consumer-sensitive, and platform-advertising exposures lag.

Energy’s 18.0 percent return was accompanied by forward P/E compression to 12.7x from 15.6x, implying approximately 45 percent forward EPS estimate growth. That is the strongest implied sector-level EPS revision in the source table. The macro risk is that a meaningful portion of the energy revision appears tied to supply disruption rather than secular demand improvement. Reuters reported on June 30 that analysts cut 2026 oil price forecasts after the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz eased supply concerns, with Brent expected to average $84.50 per barrel versus $90.44 in the prior month and WTI expected to average $79.49 versus $84.63. Reuters also reported that prices had fallen from peaks above $126 per barrel for Brent and nearly $120 for WTI as supply fears eased.  

Energy therefore looks less durable than AI infrastructure if the geopolitical risk premium continues to unwind. The sector’s earnings revisions may be real, but they can reverse quickly if production normalizes, inventories recover, demand softens, or OPEC+ raises output. By contrast, memory and AI infrastructure bottlenecks are supported by product qualification, long-term customer agreements, and physical capacity constraints, which usually unwind more slowly than oil risk premia.

PORTFOLIO IMPLICATIONS

The highest-quality long thesis in the source material is not simply “own the best performers.” The more precise conclusion is that the market is paying for supply-chain scarcity where earnings revisions are visible and where contractual structures can extend the cycle. On that basis, Micron has the most compelling combination of low forward P/E, strong EPS revision, and newly documented customer commitments. Sandisk has the most powerful earnings inflection and the rare characteristic of P/E compression despite an 858 percent stock gain, but its forward estimates are the most exposed to NAND pricing and margin normalization. Dell offers comparatively reasonable valuation and direct AI server revenue visibility, but it also faces component inflation and pass-through risk. Lumentum is notable because its EPS estimate revision exceeded its price move, although its absolute forward P/E remains elevated.

The highest valuation-risk cohort consists of Marvell, Corning, Intel, KLA, Lam Research, Teradyne, AMD, and portions of the optical/networking complex where price appreciation materially exceeded EPS revisions. These may remain strong momentum stocks if AI capex expectations continue to rise, but the margin of safety is lower. In these cases, forward earnings are not enough; the investment case requires confidence in multi-year demand duration, market share, pricing power, and terminal margin structure. The risk of paying peak multiples on peak revisions is materially higher here.

The semicap group is attractive strategically but less attractive statistically after the move. The companies benefit from AI-driven WFE, HBM, advanced packaging, NAND, and test demand, and SEMI’s forecast supports continued industry growth. However, the top-performer data indicate that equipment stocks have already capitalized a large part of that future. At roughly 54x average forward earnings for the 4 equipment/test winners, the group is vulnerable to any deceleration in orders, any memory capex discipline, any China-related export restriction pressure, or any evidence that AI infrastructure demand is being overbuilt.

The most important hedge-fund risk is factor crowding. The same factor now links memory, HDD, SSD, AI servers, networking, semicap equipment, data-center construction, and power infrastructure. It is labeled differently across sectors, but the common factor is AI physical infrastructure scarcity. A single adverse catalyst could affect many names simultaneously: a hyperscaler capex cut, AI monetization disappointment, memory price decline, supply expansion announcement, export-control shock, data-center power constraint, or reversal in retail/speculative momentum. Correlations inside the top-performer complex are likely higher than traditional sector classifications imply.

The most important fundamental risk is that supply is already responding. Micron is raising capex materially, and SEMI forecasts sustained growth in semiconductor equipment sales through 2027. Supplier discipline can extend scarcity, but high gross margins above 80 percent in memory are inherently a signal to add capacity. The likely path is not immediate oversupply, given cleanroom, node-transition, HBM, NAND, and qualification constraints. The more relevant risk is that 2027 to 2028 estimates begin to embed too much supply and too much demand persistence at the same time.

FINAL ASSESSMENT

The H1 2026 top-performer list is best interpreted as an AI infrastructure scarcity trade with a strong earnings-revision foundation but increasingly uneven valuation discipline. The broad S&P 500 move appears fundamentally supported because forward P/E compressed while prices rose. The top 20 list is more complex: Sandisk and Micron are genuinely EPS-led, Dell is supported by visible AI server demand, and Lumentum’s estimate revision is stronger than its price move. Conversely, several high-multiple hardware, networking, equipment, and turnaround names have moved far ahead of current EPS revisions.

The most defensible investment conclusion is that the market has correctly identified the bottleneck layer of AI, but not every bottleneck stock now offers attractive forward risk/reward. The portfolio opportunity is in distinguishing sustained contractual scarcity from cyclical peak earnings and separating earnings-led winners from multiple-led momentum. Memory and enterprise storage remain the central battleground. The upside case requires that AI demand, strategic customer agreements, and capacity constraints structurally reduce historical cyclicality. The downside case requires only a moderation in price increases, a supply response, or evidence that hyperscaler AI infrastructure spending is becoming less profitable. The data support continued analytical focus on the group, but they do not support indiscriminate exposure after triple-digit moves.
